zones.label: Ukraine
zones.note: Ukrainian airspace has been closed to civil aviation since the full-scale Russian invasion, so the dark cells here are the absence of civil traffic, not the absence of interference.

sources.title: EASA CZIB-2022-01 — Airspace of Ukraine
sources.note: EASA airspace bulletin; airspace closed to civil traffic.

sources.title: FlightGlobal — Ukrainian airspace closed to civil traffic (24 Feb 2022)
sources.note: UkSATSE NOTAM closed FIRs Lviv/Kyiv/Dnipro/Simferopol/Odesa at 0045Z, 24 Feb 2022.

sources.title: Safe Airspace — Ukraine
sources.note: Ongoing airspace-status reference.

zones.label: Russia
zones.note: Western carriers are barred from and avoid Russian airspace, and volunteer ADS-B receivers thin out east of the EU, so coverage here is sparse rather than absent. The visible Moscow-area blooms are domestic and Asian/Gulf carriers that still overfly — interference reads only where aircraft are actually seen.

sources.title: The Flying Engineer — Russian airspace ban: airlines, routes & impact
sources.note: Reciprocal bans from 28 Feb 2022; 35+ countries/territories barred.

sources.title: Alternative Airlines — Which airlines are still flying over Russia?
sources.note: Chinese, Gulf and domestic carriers continue to overfly (partial coverage).

sources.title: Wilson Center — Russia's civil aviation in the wake of the sanctions
sources.note: Context on sanctions and reduced Western overflight.

zones.label: Sudan
zones.note: Sudanese airspace has been closed to civil traffic (bar humanitarian and evacuation flights) since the April 2023 civil war — a data gap, not calm.

sources.title: Ops.Group — Sudan airspace closed
sources.note: Khartoum FIR closed to civil traffic; humanitarian/evac exceptions.

sources.title: FlightGlobal — Sudanese airspace closed (Apr 2023)
sources.note: Closure at the onset of the April 2023 civil war.

zones.label: Libya
zones.note: EASA advises against flying over Libya at all levels except coastal approaches from the sea, so interior traffic — and therefore coverage — is thin.

sources.title: EASA CZIB-2017-02 — Airspace of Libya
sources.note: Avoid Libyan airspace except coastal operations under stated conditions.

sources.title: Safe Airspace — Libya
sources.note: Ongoing airspace-status reference; instability since 2014.

zones.label: China Lake (NAWS), CA
zones.note: A recurring, announced US Navy GPS-testing range; China Lake's GPS-interference tests are documented in FAA advisories and press. Such tests degrade GPS for airborne aircraft (ground users are unaffected) over a footprint that widens with altitude — on a representative FAA advisory, roughly a 253 nm radius at 50 ft AGL out to about 476 nm (≈950 nm across) near 40,000 ft. Benign, scheduled, announced in advance — not interference from closed or avoided airspace.

sources.title: IEEE Spectrum — FAA Files Reveal a Surprising Threat to Airline Safety: the US Military's GPS Tests
sources.note: Documents recurring US military GPS-test advisories and their effect on civil aviation.

sources.title: GPS World — FAA issues GPS interference flight advisory
sources.note: Representative FAA GPS-test advisory geometry: ~253 nm radius at 50 ft AGL, widening with altitude to ~476 nm radius (≈950 nm across) at FL400.

zones.label: White Sands (WSMR), NM
zones.note: A recurring, announced US Army GPS-testing range (White Sands Missile Range). FAA flight advisories degrade GPS over a broad radius during scheduled tests, published in advance and coordinated with civil aviation.

sources.title: FAA/FAAST — WSMR GPS Interference Flight Advisory (WSMRNM 26-13)
sources.note: Primary FAA flight advisory for a scheduled White Sands GPS test.

sources.title: FAA — GPS/GNSS Interference Resource Guide
sources.note: FAA reference on announced GNSS-test advisories and NOTAMs.

zones.label: North Korea
zones.note: Civil traffic over North Korea is minimal — the FAA prohibits US operators throughout the Pyongyang FIR (SFAR 79) and most states avoid it over unannounced missile launches — so volunteer ADS-B coverage inside the country is effectively nil. It is the archive's starkest sensor desert: North Korea is a named interference actor here (see the korea region), yet its own territory shows nothing — the jamming is observable only where the sensors are, in South Korean airspace.

sources.title: 14 CFR 91.1615 (SFAR 79) — Prohibition against certain flights in the Pyongyang FIR
sources.note: FAA prohibits US operators throughout the Pyongyang FIR; in force through 18 Sep 2028.

sources.title: Safe Airspace — North Korea
sources.note: US prohibits all North Korean airspace (incl. oceanic); missile-debris / misidentification risk.

zones.label: Afghanistan
zones.note: The Kabul FIR lost air traffic services after the August 2021 withdrawal and is uncontrolled; the FAA prohibits US operators below FL320 (SFAR 119) and most Western carriers avoid it, though some high-altitude corridor traffic has returned. Low-altitude coverage is effectively absent.

sources.title: 14 CFR 91.1619 (SFAR 119) — Prohibition against certain flights in the Kabul FIR
sources.note: US operators prohibited below FL320; high-altitude overflight permitted with conditions.

sources.title: Safe Airspace — Afghanistan
sources.note: Uncontrolled Class G since Aug 2021; no ATC; low-altitude SAM risk; high-altitude overflights ongoing.

zones.label: Yemen
zones.note: Yemeni airspace is avoided under active-conflict advisories — the FAA prohibits US operators over the landmass (SFAR 115) and EASA rates the Sanaa FIR high-risk at all altitudes — so the interior stays dark while the adjacent Red Sea shows bright offshore interference: signal where the sensors are, blindness where they are not.

sources.title: Safe Airspace — Yemen
sources.note: Level 1 'Do Not Fly'; FAA prohibition over the landmass through Jan 2028; EASA HIGH.

sources.title: EASA CZIB-2017-07 — Airspace of Yemen (Sana'a FIR)
sources.note: EASA rates the Sana'a FIR HIGH risk at all altitudes.

zones.label: Iran
zones.note: A longstanding FAA prohibition covers the entire Tehran FIR (SFAR 117, since 2020 — its rationale explicitly cites GPS and communications jammers), and the airspace has been intermittently closed during the 2025-2026 escalations. Coverage here is thin and episodic — never a stable open/closed state.

sources.title: 14 CFR 91.1617 (SFAR 117) — Prohibition against certain flights in the Tehran FIR
sources.note: FAA prohibits US operators throughout the Tehran FIR (since 2020); rationale cites GPS/comms jammers.

sources.title: Safe Airspace — Iran
sources.note: Level 1; episodic closures across 2025-2026 (Mar 2026 full closure; Jun 2026 partial eastern reopen).
